title: Procedure Development
narrationScript: Here is an uncomfortable truth about magnetic particle testing. The method has so many adjustable knobs, current type, amperage, field direction, particle system, lighting, timing, that two skilled technicians left to improvise can run two very different tests on the same part, and both will call it inspected. The written procedure exists to close that gap. In this lesson we look at how procedures are developed, what feeds into them, and how we prove they work before we trust them. Start with why standards exist at all. A standard gives everyone, your shop, your customer, and the auditor, a common definition of an acceptable test. It carries the lessons of decades of failures into your booth. And it settles arguments, because when the contract names the document, the document decides. The paperwork stacks up in a hierarchy. At the top sits the design code or the contract, which tells you when testing is required and what the acceptance criteria are. Below that, industry practices, the widely used ASTM documents for magnetic particle testing and the ASME code articles, define what a proper test looks like. Your company's written procedure translates those requirements onto your equipment and your parts, and it is developed and approved at Level Three. At the bottom, the technique card carries the exact settings for one part number. As a Level Two you execute the procedure and the card, and your feedback flows back up when something does not work on the bench. Developing a procedure starts with the part, and the part asks five questions. First, what is its history. New production, or in service with prior repairs and prior findings on record. A repaired area or a past crack location gets special attention. Second, how was it manufactured. Casting, rolling, forging, and welding each write their own list of expected discontinuities and orientations, and that list chooses your field directions. Third, what could cause a defect. Process induced flaws born at manufacture, or service induced cracking at fillets, threads, keyways, and oil holes where stress concentrates. Fourth, how is the part used. The stress state and the consequence of failure decide how much coverage and how much sensitivity the procedure must buy. A garden tractor axle and a helicopter rotor component do not get the same examination. Fifth, what are the acceptance criteria. They come from the design code or the customer, and they must exist before the first part is tested, never invented afterward. Now a word about numbers. Values in a procedure are written as windows, not single points, and that is deliberate. An amperage called out as a single value is unworkable, because real machines drift and real operators cannot hold a knob to the ampere. So procedures state ranges and tolerances. A shot current with a plus or minus band, commonly about ten percent. A tangential field window on the order of thirty to sixty gauss when measured with a Hall effect meter. A bath concentration range, such as one tenth to four tenths of a milliliter per hundred milliliters for fluorescent particles. Prod spacing between six and eight inches. And the coil rule of thumb, ampere turns equal to forty five thousand divided by the length to diameter ratio, valid roughly for ratios between two and fifteen. Tolerances are what make a procedure executable, repeatable, and auditable. But a calculated number is only a starting point, and that is where validation comes in. We prove the technique on the real geometry. Tape artificial flaw shims, the notched foils commonly called QQIs, notch side toward the part, at the locations that matter, including the worst case corners and section changes. Run the technique with the continuous method. If the notches bloom cleanly, the field is adequate at those spots, and a Hall probe reading confirms the window. Where they exist, parts with known natural defects are gold. System level checks, like a ring specimen run on a central conductor, confirm that the whole chain, machine, particles, and lights, is performing. The demonstration is witnessed, captured in a qualification record, and the procedure is approved at Level Three. After that it is a controlled document. Change the equipment, the particle system, the current type, the part geometry, or the coverage, and you validate again. The procedure is not paperwork for its own sake. It is the memory of every hard lesson this method has learned, written down so the next shift does not have to learn it twice.
imagePrompt: An experienced inspector and a younger technician review a draft technique sheet on a clipboard beside a wet horizontal bench unit, a forged steel shaft clamped between copper-braid contact heads in a fluorescent-lit shop.

sections.heading: Why the Method Lives on Paper
sections.body:
MT offers more adjustable parameters than almost any surface method: current type, amperage, field direction, particle system, application timing, lighting. Freedom is flexibility — and variability. Without a controlling document, sensitivity drifts from shift to shift and shop to shop, and nobody can say afterward what the examination actually proved.

**The need for standards and references** comes down to four things:

- **Repeatability** — the same part, tested next month or in another facility, gets an equivalent examination.
- **A common definition of 'good enough'** — customer, shop, and auditor all read the same requirements.
- **Accumulated experience** — standard practices encode decades of failure analysis, so you do not rediscover prod arc strikes or missed transverse cracks the hard way.
- **Dispute resolution and auditability** — when the contract names the document, the document decides, and objective evidence shows you followed it.

For a Level II this is practical, not philosophical: the procedure tells you what to set, what to verify, and what to record — and protects your call when an indication becomes a disagreement.

sections.caption: The MT document hierarchy: requirements flow down from the design code or contract through standard practices to the company procedure and part technique card, while results and feedback flow back up.

sections.heading: Using Standards: From Code to Technique Card
sections.body:
Knowing **which document answers which question** is a Level II skill.

- **Design codes and contracts** (for example, boiler and pressure vessel work governed by documents such as ASME Section V with its referencing code sections, or an aerospace prime's specification) answer *when* MT is required, *how much* coverage, and *what acceptance criteria* apply.
- **Standard practices** (documents such as ASTM E1444 for ferromagnetic-part examination, with ASTM E709 as the broad guide) answer *how* a valid examination is performed — equipment checks, lighting, particle controls, technique requirements.
- **The company written procedure** translates those requirements onto your shop's specific equipment, parts, and forms. It is developed and approved by a Level III, and where contracts require it, accepted by the customer or certifying body.
- **The technique card** (or technique sheet) carries the exact, validated settings for one part number: fixturing, shots, amperages, coverage zones.

Editions matter — the contract invokes specific revisions, and a procedure citing the wrong year can fail an audit even when the bench work is flawless. When requirements conflict, the order of precedence stated in the contract and procedure decides.

sections.heading: Part Considerations: History, Process, and Defect Causes
sections.body:
A procedure is built around the part, and the first three inputs are its biography.

- **History of the part (7.3.1).** New production or in-service? An overhauled component carries service history: prior repairs, weld buildups, previously documented indications. Repaired zones and past crack sites get targeted coverage, and in-service parts may need paint stripping, pre-demagnetization, or both before testing.
- **Manufacturing process (7.3.2).** The product form writes the expected-discontinuity list — castings: shrinkage, hot tears, cold shuts; wrought: seams, laps, stringers, bursts; welds: toe cracks, lack of fusion, underbead cracking. That list, with each flaw's expected **orientation**, chooses the field directions and current type the procedure must specify.
- **Possible causes of defect (7.3.3).** Think in two bins. *Process-induced* defects exist when the part arrives — they are found once, at the right manufacturing stage. *Service-induced* defects (fatigue at fillets, threads, keyways, and oil holes; corrosion-assisted cracking) initiate at stress concentrations and grow — they drive recurring-inspection procedures aimed precisely at those locations.

A procedure that ignores the part's biography inspects everything in general and nothing in particular.

sections.heading: Usage and Acceptance Criteria
sections.body:
**Usage of the part (7.3.4)** sets the stakes. Where does the stress concentrate, and what happens if the part fails? A highly loaded rotating component with fatigue exposure justifies tighter coverage, higher sensitivity (wet fluorescent rather than dry visible), and tighter field-strength verification than a static bracket. Critical zones — fillet radii, splines, lug holes — may be called out for dedicated shots even when the rest of the part gets general coverage. Consequence of failure also drives how often in-service parts return for reexamination.

**Acceptance/rejection criteria (7.3.5)** are the procedure's destination. They come from the design code, specification, or customer order — never from the inspector's judgment after the fact — and they must be in hand **before** the technique is developed, because they size the flaw the technique must reliably find. If the criteria reject any relevant linear indication in a fillet, the validated field strength, particle system, and lighting must demonstrably show such an indication at that location. Criteria written as classifications (linear versus rounded, by zone, by length) also dictate what the recording section of the procedure must capture: location, orientation, dimensions, and classification of every relevant indication.

sections.heading: Tolerances Make It Executable
sections.body:
Single-point values are unworkable on a real bench — machines drift, line voltage sags, operators cannot hold a knob to the ampere. So a well-developed procedure states every controlling parameter as a **value with a tolerance** or an acceptable **window**:

- **Amperage:** the established shot current with a band — commonly on the order of ±10%.
- **Tangential field strength:** a window such as 30–60 gauss measured with a Hall-effect meter, where the procedure uses field measurement.
- **Bath concentration:** 0.1–0.4 mL per 100 mL for fluorescent, 1.2–2.4 mL for visible (typical settling-test windows).
- **Geometry-driven ranges:** prod spacing commonly 6–8 inches; the low-fill-factor coil rule NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D) applied only within its validity range (L/D roughly 2–15).
- **Timing:** minimum shot duration (commonly about half a second) and minimum number of shots.

Tolerances serve three masters: the **operator**, who can actually set and hold the value; the **verifier**, who can check compliance with a meter; and the **auditor**, who can see objective evidence that the process stayed inside its qualified window. A parameter without a tolerance is an argument waiting to happen.

sections.title: Formulas are starting points — validation is proof
sections.body: Rules of thumb such as **NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D)** for low-fill-factor coils or head-shot amperage per inch of diameter get you onto the bench, nothing more. The qualified values in the procedure are the ones **demonstrated** to produce adequate field — QQI shims blooming at the worst-case locations, Hall-probe readings inside the window — and then written down **with tolerances**. Your written procedure governs over any rule of thumb.

sections.caption: Validating a technique: a QQI artificial-flaw shim taped notch-side down at a representative location, with a Hall-effect probe confirming the tangential field window.

sections.heading: Validation: Prove It Finds What It Must
sections.body:
**Validation (7.5)** is the demonstration that the drafted technique actually produces adequate, properly oriented fields and detects the target flaws.

- **Artificial-flaw shims (QQIs):** thin notched foils taped **notch side toward the part** at representative locations — always including worst-case geometry such as fillets, section changes, and the ends of coil-magnetized regions. Clean particle bloom over the notches with the continuous method demonstrates adequate field at that spot and in that direction.
- **Hall-effect gaussmeter:** puts a number on the tangential surface field (procedures commonly target on the order of 30–60 G), confirming and recording the window the shims demonstrated.
- **Known-defect parts:** components with documented natural discontinuities are the most convincing demonstration pieces where they exist.
- **System verification:** a ring-specimen run (central conductor, counting visible hole indications at specified amperages) proves the whole chain — machine, bath, lights — performs.

The demonstration is witnessed, recorded in a **procedure qualification record**, and the procedure is approved (Level III, plus customer where required). It then becomes a **controlled document**: any change to equipment, particle system, current type, part geometry, or coverage triggers **revalidation**. Routine daily QC checks, by contrast, only confirm the qualified system is still behaving — they are the subject of the next lesson.

figures.title: Technique validation with QQI shim and Hall probe

interactive.title: Build the Technique: A New Part Hits Your Bench
interactive.intro: Your shop wins work on a machined steel pump shaft — new production, 12 inches long, 2 inches in diameter, with a fillet radius the designer flags as the highest-stressed feature. You are assisting the Level III in developing the procedure and technique card.

steps.prompt: Before anything touches the bench, what do you pull first?
options: Last year's technique card for a different shaft — it is probably close enough
The governing code or customer specification, including its acceptance criteria and the standard practice it invokes
The amperage tables from an old textbook
steps.feedback: Development starts at the top of the hierarchy: the contract or design code defines when, how much, and against what criteria. The technique exists to satisfy those criteria — you cannot design it before you have them.

steps.prompt: The shaft is machined from hot-rolled bar and will see rotating-bending fatigue in service. What does that history tell you about field directions?
options: One circular shot is enough — bar stock only has seams
Expect longitudinal flaws (seams, stringers) from the bar plus transverse fatigue cracking at the fillet in service — so the technique needs both circular and longitudinal (coil) magnetization
Skip circular magnetization since the part is machined
steps.feedback: Manufacturing process predicts longitudinal discontinuities, which circular magnetization crosses; usage predicts transverse fatigue cracks at the fillet, which a coil shot crosses. Two roughly perpendicular magnetizations cover both.

steps.prompt: For the coil shot, L/D = 6 gives NI ≈ 45,000/6 = 7,500 ampere-turns — 1,500 A on the 5-turn coil. How does this number enter the procedure?
options: As the final qualified value — the formula is the proof
As a starting point: verify with QQI shims at the fillet and shaft ends plus a Hall-probe reading, then write the demonstrated value with a tolerance
It doesn't — coil shots cannot be toleranced
steps.feedback: Rules of thumb start the development; validation finishes it. The card carries the demonstrated amperage with a band (commonly about ±10%), backed by shim response at worst-case locations and a recorded field reading.

steps.prompt: During validation, the QQI at the fillet barely blooms while the mid-span shim shows clearly. What is the correct development move?
options: Accept the technique — one good shim is enough
Adjust the technique (amperage, coil position, or an added shot) until the worst-case location demonstrates adequate field, then re-verify and record
Move the shim to a flatter spot where it blooms better
steps.feedback: Validation lives at the worst-case location — that fillet is the highest-stressed feature and the reason for the examination. You qualify the technique to the hardest spot, never relocate the evidence to an easier one.

steps.prompt: Six months later the shop replaces the bench unit with a different model. The procedure and card are unchanged. What is required?
options: Nothing — the paperwork is still valid
Revalidation: demonstrate the technique on the new equipment (shims/field readings), update the qualification record, and re-approve
Only a new coat of paint on the technique card
steps.feedback: A procedure is qualified as a system — equipment included. Changing the machine, particle system, current type, geometry, or coverage triggers revalidation and re-approval before production testing resumes.

checkQuestions.question: What is the primary reason MT procedures are built on published standards and references?
options: Standards eliminate the need for trained personnel
They provide a repeatable, auditable definition of an acceptable examination that customer, shop, and auditor all share
They guarantee every flaw will be found regardless of technique
They exist mainly to make examinations slower
checkQuestions.explanation: Standards give everyone the same definition of a valid test, carry accumulated industry experience, and provide the objective basis for audits and dispute resolution — they support skilled personnel rather than replace them.

checkQuestions.question: A part has L/D = 6 and the procedure uses a low-fill-factor coil. The rule of thumb gives NI ≈ 45,000/6 = 7,500 ampere-turns. How should this value be treated during procedure development?
options: As the final qualified value, since the formula is in published guides
As a starting point to be verified with QQI shims and Hall-probe readings at worst-case locations, then written into the procedure with a tolerance
As an absolute maximum that may never be exceeded
As irrelevant, because coils cannot magnetize parts with L/D above 3
checkQuestions.explanation: The 45,000/(L/D) rule (valid roughly for L/D between 2 and 15) only seeds the development; the qualified value is the one demonstrated by shim response and field measurement, recorded with a tolerance.

checkQuestions.question: Where do the acceptance/rejection criteria in an MT procedure come from?
options: The inspector's experience, applied after looking at the indications
The particle manufacturer's data sheet
The governing design code, specification, or customer order — established before testing begins
Whatever the previous shift used
checkQuestions.explanation: Criteria flow down from the design code or customer and must pre-exist the test, because the technique is developed and validated specifically to find what those criteria reject.

checkQuestions.question: Why does a well-written procedure state amperage as a value with a tolerance (for example, 1,000 A ± 10%) rather than a single point?
options: To allow the operator to pick any current they like
Because real equipment drifts and operators cannot hold exact values — a toleranced window keeps the process executable, verifiable, and auditable while staying inside the qualified range
Because tolerances are required only for fluorescent particles
To compensate for the earth's magnetic field
checkQuestions.explanation: Tolerances acknowledge real-world drift while bounding the process to conditions that were validated — the operator can set it, the verifier can measure it, and the auditor can confirm it.

checkQuestions.question: Which change does NOT by itself trigger revalidation of a qualified MT procedure?
options: Replacing the bench unit with a different model
Switching from fluorescent wet particles to visible dry powder
A routine daily settling test reading inside its normal window
Adding a new part geometry with different L/D and fillet locations
checkQuestions.explanation: Routine QC checks that pass simply confirm the qualified system is still behaving; changes to equipment, particle system, current type, geometry, or coverage alter the qualified system itself and require revalidation.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §7.1–7.2 (use of and need for standards and references)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §7.3 (part considerations: history, manufacturing, defect causes, usage, acceptance criteria)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §7.4–7.5 (use of tolerances, validation processes)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V, Article 7 (procedure requirements and qualification — general practice)
